note_data.title: Electrochemistry
keyPoints: **Electrolysis** is the decomposition of an ionic compound using electrical energy when molten or in aqueous solution
During electrolysis, **cations** move to the cathode (negative electrode) and **anions** move to the anode (positive electrode)
At the cathode, **reduction** occurs (gain of electrons) and at the anode, **oxidation** occurs (loss of electrons)
In the electrolysis of water, **hydrogen gas** is produced at the cathode and **oxygen gas** is produced at the anode
The **volume of hydrogen** produced is twice the volume of oxygen because the ratio is 2H₂ : O₂
**Electrical conductivity** occurs in metals due to mobile electrons and in ionic solutions due to mobile ions
A **fuel cell** converts chemical energy directly into electrical energy using hydrogen and oxygen
In a hydrogen fuel cell, hydrogen is **oxidized** at the anode and oxygen is **reduced** at the cathode
Electrolysis can be used for **electroplating** metals and **extraction** of reactive metals like aluminum

definitions.definition: The decomposition of an ionic compound using electrical energy when the compound is molten or in aqueous solution
A substance that conducts electricity when molten or in aqueous solution and is decomposed by the electric current
A device that converts chemical energy directly into electrical energy using hydrogen and oxygen
A conductor through which electricity enters or leaves an electrolyte during electrolysis

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a detailed account including what happens at each electrode
Use discharge rules to determine products at each electrode

modelAnswer.question: Describe what happens during the electrolysis of dilute sulfuric acid using platinum electrodes. Include the electrode reactions.
modelAnswer.answer: Water molecules are decomposed. At the cathode, hydrogen ions gain electrons to form hydrogen gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂. At the anode, hydroxide ions lose electrons to form oxygen gas and water: 4OH⁻ → O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻. Hydrogen gas is produced at the cathode and oxygen gas is produced at the anode. The volume of hydrogen is twice the volume of oxygen. The solution becomes more concentrated as water is removed.

commonMistakes: Never say 'electricity is conducted by atoms' — always say 'electricity is conducted by mobile electrons in metals or mobile ions in ionic solutions'
Students often confuse which electrode is positive/negative — remember cathode is negative, anode is positive
Never write H₂SO₄ as being discharged in dilute acid — it's always H⁺ and OH⁻ from water
Students forget to balance electrode equations or omit electrons completely
Don't confuse fuel cells with electrolysis — fuel cells produce electricity, electrolysis uses electricity
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2, especially electrolysis of water and simple salts
Always state the products at each electrode clearly and write balanced equations
Remember discharge rules: less reactive elements are discharged first
Draw and label electrolysis diagrams showing electron flow and ion movement
In Pakistan context: electroplating is used in Sialkot sports goods industry for metal coating
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Electrolysis cell showing two electrodes in electrolyte, with battery/power supply, electron flow from anode to cathode through external circuit, and ion movement in solution (cations to cathode, anions to anode)
